--- SOURCE 1: [PDF] Public Policies to Support the Agroecological Transition ---
URL: https://www.coordinationsud.org/wp-content/uploads/notes_19_anglais-1.pdf

SUMMARY:
Promoting and supporting peasant-based agroecology involves making public policies consistent. Models that harm agroecology cannot be supported at the same time

FULL CONTENT:
1 N°19 THE OF Public Policies to Support the Agroecological Transition © élodie Perriot/SCCF a g r i c u l t u r e • F O O D Of NOTES # N°19 Coordination SUD July 2019 Promoting and supporting peasant-based agroecology involves making public policies consistent. Models that harm agroecology cannot be supported at the same time. Agroecology is a model that fits in with the territory where it is practiced. It is developed according to the environment and the knowledge of the local populations, and this dependence on its environment calls for holistic reflection.
A gro-industrial systems have reached their limits. They are responsible for one-third of green-house gases1 and lead to significant environ-mental degradation (water and soil pollution, collapse of biodiversity) and serious health consequences that include a social disaster causing the marginali-zation of huge swathes of the population. Above all, they are unsuccessful in feeding humanity properly: 821 million people worldwide suffered from hunger in 2017, a tragic situation once again on the upswing. Meanwhile, obesity is continuing to grow, affecting 13.2% of adults worldwide in 2016.2 Faced with the failure of the agro-industrial model, a transition to agroeco-logy would help overcome the various aforementioned limits by overhauling food systems to achieve a sustain-able model that can feed people. This view is becoming increasingly shared, both locally (in particular by peasant organizations) and in the policies of some governments, and it is surfacing in international bodies such as the FAO. Coordination SUD proposes a set of recommendations for supporting the agroecological transition. While there’s no single solution, the recommendations below outline various and often complementary approaches that can respond to the key issues of the transition and that will have to be adapted to national and territorial contexts. >>> 1. Emile Frison, De l’uniformité à la diversité, IPES-Food, June 2016 2. FAO, The State of Food and Agriculture, 2018.
THE SUD 2 N°19 THE OF 3. Land security is interpreted by the Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights as a component to the right of food. Consideration of Reports Submitted by States Parties under Articles 16 and 17 of the Covenant: Concluding Observations of the Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights: Madagascar, UN doc. E/C.12/MDG/CO/2.
4. On this topic, see: carbonmarketwatch.org/publications/practitioners-guide-for-local-stakeholder-consultation-how-to-ensure-adequate-participation-in-climate-mitigation-actions/ 1 Direct financing toward the agroecological transition and peasant-based agroecology Transforming food systems involves starting up a change in model. Public and private financing allocated to agriculture must be redirected to support for peasant-based agroecology.
Public policies must promote the acquisition of other means of production such as agricultural equipment and organic fertilizers, which are crucial levers for the agro-ecological transition. Moreover, subsidies for pesticides distort prices and do not give incentive to the transition.
2 Transform governance The development of agroecology is eminently linked to political and democratic transformation, especially at the local level. The agroecological transition is inseperable from respecting people’s rights, strengthening their power to act, and enhancing their knowledge.
Promote the approach through rights Access to water, land, and seeds is one of the undeni-able prerequisites for agroecology. Such access is essen-tial for achieving the right to food3 and must be governed according to an approach through rights. Peasant seeds must circulate freely among peasants, without being privatized or patented. Because agroecology is based on the conservation and development of diversity, seeds must be excluded from trade agreements. Exchange and sharing of seeds must be promoted by seed banks and seed fairs.
Recognizing the right to land is also necessary. This can involve different approaches depending on national legislative contexts, local customary rights, and past and present agrarian reforms. It’s essential to be able to obtain land deeds and land tenure, without gender discrimination, in order to improve land sustainably and reach production of sufficient quantity and quality.
While the right to land is a prerequisite for the devel-opment of agroecology, integrating it into public policies can imply redistributive agrarian reforms. Clarification of private property deeds according to the Western notion is a possible solution, but a certain number of particular aspects must be taken into account. While the current dominant framework admittedly makes it possible to clarify individual or—more rarely—collective rights to land, this approach also encourages investments: those with the means buy, and those lacking funds sell. Sometimes it’s better to look at customary and collective practices and systems, some cate-gories of which allow for indivisible or non-transferable plots, without needing to enter into market dynamics. It’s nevertheless necessary to take into account discriminations that certain groups, such as women, might be subject to within the systems and remedy them.
The Declaration on the Rights of Peasants, adopted by the United Nations in December 2018, recognizes the rights to land, seeds, water, and food sovereignty. This recognition is crucial, because the agricultural transition cannot be carried out by peasants alone.
Finally, States must support the United Nations Committee on World Food Security as an inclusive and legitimate governance platform that promotes the approach through rights.
Strengthen local communities’ power to act in managing commons Greater reflection is taking place on the theme of the governance of “commons,” not only among researchers, but also in development institutions such as Agence Française de Développement. The issue is one of changing the basic paradigm, in order to think no longer in terms of owners but in terms of decisions and management.
The issue of land especially raises questions of strong governance that go hand-in-hand with the participative processes inherent to agroecology. Peasants’ power to act can be increased by organizing spaces of dialog, exchange, and participation. In doing so, agroecology enables a trans-formation of power relations and offers new structures of power that are decentralized and adapted locally. To achieve this, it’s crucial to recognize the right to consulta-tion and to Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) for all the inhabitants of the territory, and to extend it to beyond just indigenous peoples.4 3 Make food systems rooted locally Upscaling agroecology is possible only if producers can sell their production on local markets, within short circuits. A number of stimuli to develop agroecology have to be activated at the local level, but in doing so the creation of legislative and administrative framework to promote these initiatives should not be neglected.
Support local initiatives for economic development Support for networking and for the formation of peasant organizations, cooperatives, or other collective organiza-tions is essential. This helps guarantee fair remuneration as well as direct management by peasants of their produc-tion and outlets.
Development of storage, transport, and processing infrastructure at the local level also helps strengthen markets and improve food sovereignty locally. Give priority to short circuits and fair trade The marketing of products on local markets must be strengthened, by helping peasants to network with consumers and sell their production. This helps eliminate the need for intermediaries and contributes to local food and nutritional security, all the while limiting imported products.
3 N°19 THE OF 5. Bruno Parmentier, Faim Zéro. En finir avec la faim dans le monde, La Découverte, 2014 6. See, for example: www.inter-reseaux.org/publications/revue-grain-de-sel/48-mecanisation-et-motorisation/article/la-regulation-du-marche-de-l 7. IPCC, 2018: “Summary for Policymakers”. In: Global Warming of 1.5°C. IPCC Special Report on the impacts of global warming of 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.
8. émile Frison, De l’uniformité à la diversité, IPES-Food, June 2016 There must also be significant work to sensitize peasant organizations and the local population with regard to furthering local production and consumption. This comes with strengthening cohesion in the local area.
The development of public procurement from small-holder peasants working along agroecological principles can contribute to its growth. In Brazil, the Zero Hunger program helped spread a system making it possible for schools to be supplied from family farmers, by having the State pay 30% more for the products if they were produced through agroecology.5 Here we find the obvious link between agroecology and food security, though it is impor-tant that producers do not lose their food sovereignty if the State imposes types of crops when it places orders.
Furthermore, enhancement of agroecological products must also be accompanied by work to put peasant organ-izations in relation with markets for quality products, thanks to value chains for organic, diversified, and fair products. The particularity of such value chains is that they offer more remunerative prices all the while strengthening the influence of peasant organizations in the value chains concerned.
Protect peasant networks against competition from international trade Large-scale implementation of agroecology requires not just a favorable economic environment: it’s also impor-tant to limit competition from products that are imported at low cost (because they are subsidized and benefit from very low customs duties) and that destabilize local markets. This requires, at the international and national levels, an overhaul of international trade policies by guaranteeing that the sovereignty of developing countries is respected when trade agreements are designed and implemented, and by placing special attention on food sovereignty. It’s also important to ensure that peasant organizations are involved in the design, implementation, and moni-toring of these agreements. In Senegal, for example, since the government froze imports of certain foods (poultry, onions, potatoes, and rice), national production has grown considerably.6 Adopt a holistic and local approach The holistic approach required for the agroecological transition also involves political measures that affect other sectors at the local level. Public policies that promote the spread of agroecology are policies that not only take into account the aspects of support to a practice or an agricul-tural system, but that also enhance development and a life of dignity in the local area. The approach must be multi-faceted and include education; the creation of economic opportunities in the area; respect and promotion of local crop varieties, animal species, and seeds; environmental impacts (including on biodiversity) locally; and, finally, food sovereignty.
4 Climate change as an opportunity to seize upon The climate change we are now experiencing poses multiple challenges that the agricultural and food sector must start taking up starting now. While the implementa-tion of policies to fight climate change represents a chal-lenge, it’s also a great occasion to rethink all of our food systems, to make them rooted locally again, and to make them more equitable and fairer. Response to this challenge is urgent, as the IPCC report on the impacts of global warming of 1.5°C has put the spotlight on the effect of our food habits and the importance of transforming our life-styles if we want to maintain the objectives set by the Paris Agreement.7 Indeed, our food systems represent nearly one-third of GHS emissions.8 >>> © élodie Perriot/SCCF 4 N°19 THE OF C2A publications are produced with the support of the AFD. The viewpoints expressed in this document in no way represent the official point of view of the AFD.
Implement the Paris Agreement by supporting the agroecological transition In the Paris Agreement, governments undertook to contain the rise in average temperature of the planet to below 2°C by the end of the century, and as close as possible to 1.5°C. The instruments for implementing this objective are the Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs), in which each State proposes a national scenario for emis-sion reduction and possibly for adaptation, across sectors. The accumulated NDCs must make it possible to maintain the Paris Agreement objectives according to a bottom-up approach. It is essential to have a food-systems approach within the NDCs, which must therefore formulate tran-sition methods for national food systems, in particular by supporting small-scale peasant agriculture and the agroe-cological transition of agricultural systems. The objective is to guarantee the food security of the poorest populations and to preserve the environment for current and future generations.
Climate financing for adaptation must also be increased and directed to peasants: in 2016, such financing repre-sented less than 20% of total climate financing.9 5 Transform evaluation indicators A key element in developing public policies is the choice of evaluation indicators. By focusing on Gross Domestic Product, the Human Development Index (HDI), or agri-cultural production volumes, public policies unavoidably become oriented solely toward economic productivity objectives. However, what responds to economic performance objectives does not take into account food security, funda-mental rights, environmental impacts, or the well-being of local populations.
Furthermore, there is a lack of data on farmers who produce according to agroecological principles and on the family-farm sector in general. This leads to a bias when public policies are designed.
The evaluation indicators must reflect a holistic approach to food systems and take into account a group of dimensions at the local level, without negative impact on local populations and the environment. Furthermore, the public policies enacted must specifically aim for improve-ment of these indicators.
● From a social point of view, the indicators that orient public policies must take into account the four dimensions of food security (availability of food, physical and economic access, nutritional and health quality, and regularity of these three previous dimensions), access to land, water, training and financial services, as well as public health.
● From an environmental point of view, it is becoming crucial to include sustainability indicators: methane emis-sions, nitrous oxide and carbon dioxide that stem from practices, conservation and restoration of soil fertility, water pollution and consumption, biodiversity, integrity of ecosystems, etc.
● From a political point of view, the power of people to act, and in particular women’s participation in decision-making, must be taken into account when evaluating the indicators of whether agroecological food systems operate properly. A good public policy for agroecology must support peasant initiatives and encourage their creation, without taking the leadership in implementing agricultural projects. It’s thus a question of encouraging the emergence of community initi-atives by creating a favorable environment.
● Finally, from an economic point of view, quantita-tive and qualitative indicators must evaluate the impact of public policies, taking into account the jobs created and present income (which must be decent) as much as profit-ability and productivity. This publication is produced by the Agriculture and food Commission (C2A) of Coordination SUD.
As part of its mission to support the collective ad-vocacy of its members, Coordination SUD has set up working committees. The Agriculture and food Commission (C2A) brings together international solidarity NGOs working to realize the right to food and increase support for smallholder farming in policies that impact world food security: ActionAid France, Action Contre la Faim, Agter, Artisans du Monde, AVSF, CARI, CCFD - Terre Solidaire, CFSI, Commerce Équitable France, Gret, Iram, ISF Agrista, MADERA, Max Havelaar, Oxfam France, Réseau foi et Justice Afrique Europe, Secours Catholique - Caritas France, SOL and UNMFREO.
The C2A is in charge of the representation of Coordination SUD to institutions dealing with agriculture and food, such as the Interministerial Group on Food Security (GISA) and the Civil Society Mechanism (CSM) for the Committee on World Food Security (CFS).
Contact Agriculture and food commission: Carline Mainenti (AVSF) Email: c.mainenti@avsf.org Website: www.coordinationsud.org Written by Sara Lickel (Secours Catholique - Caritas France), with contributions from Katia Roesch (AVSF), Hélène Botreau (Oxfam), and Laurent Levard (GRET) éDITEUR COORDINATION SUD 14 passage Dubail 75010 Paris Tél. : 01 44 72 93 72 Directeur de publication : Philippe Jahshan Coordinatrice : Bénédicte Bimoko Impression sur papier recyclé par l’Imprimerie des MFR Dépôt légal à parution ISSN 2558-6815 9. Oxfam International, Climate Finance Shadow Report, May 2018

--- SOURCE 2: How Can Policy Support Agroecological Transitions Globally? ---
URL: https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/question/how-can-policy-support-agroecological-transitions-globally/

SUMMARY:
Direct policy support is essential to create a level playing field and incentivize the adoption of agroecological practices Meaning →

FULL CONTENT:
![Climate → Sustainability Directory](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Climate-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Climate → Sustainability Directory](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Climate-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![How Can Policy Support Agroecological Transitions Globally?](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/how-can-policy-support-agroecological-transitions-globally-question-image.webp)

# How Can Policy Support Agroecological Transitions Globally?

![An abstract sculpture using natural colors embodies themes of environmental sustainability and harmony. Spherical shapes representing planets and organic branching forms suggest interconnectedness, habitat, and balance. The use of eco friendly materials evokes responsible consumption, and highlights the urgent need for climate action, carbon sequestration, resilience, adaptation, renewable energy, pollution control, and reduction in environmental degradation to build a future fostering planetary health.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-sculpture-reflecting-climate-action-and-environmental-harmony.jpg "Abstract Sculpture Reflecting Climate Action and Environmental Harmony Representing Interconnectedness and Sustainable Growth with Climate Resilience")

## Fundamentals

Agroecology, at its heart, is a way of farming that works **with nature**, not against it. Imagine agriculture that mimics natural ecosystems, focusing on diversity, synergy, and resilience. It’s about producing food while also protecting our planet, supporting communities, and ensuring fair livelihoods. But shifting from conventional, [industrial agriculture Meaning → Industrial Agriculture: Large-scale, intensive farming focused on maximizing output and efficiency through specialization, mechanization, and standardization.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/industrial-agriculture/)  to agroecology isn’t automatic.

It requires deliberate and thoughtful policy support. Think of policy as the **architect of change**, designing the structures and incentives needed for this transition to happen on a global scale.

Policies are the blueprints for agroecological transitions, guiding and incentivizing the shift towards sustainable food systems.

For someone just starting to understand this, the first question might be → why do we even need policy to support agroecology? Isn’t it just about farmers choosing to farm differently? The reality is that the current food system is heavily shaped by existing policies that, often unintentionally, favor industrial agriculture.

Subsidies, research funding, trade agreements → many of these are geared towards large-scale, monoculture farming that relies heavily on synthetic inputs. Agroecology, to truly take root and spread, needs policies that level the playing field and actively **promote its principles**.

![A symbolic earth sphere illustrates the fragility of our planet in the face of climate change, showing a planet marred by fractures and resource depletion. Evoking the urgency for environmental sustainability, plumes of blue smoke rise, reminding us of atmospheric pollution from greenhouse gas emissions. This image stresses the significance of renewable energy adoption, a smaller carbon footprint, and climate action against ecological damage like desertification and ecosystem imbalance for the planet's resilience.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/fragile-earth-sphere-depicting-climate-change-impacts-and-need-for.jpg "Fragile Earth Sphere Depicting Climate Change Impacts and Need for Environmental Sustainability Measures")

### What Policies Can Directly Support Agroecology?

Direct support means policies that are specifically designed to encourage agroecological practices. These can take many forms:

Direct policy support is essential to create a level playing field and incentivize the adoption of agroecological practices by farmers worldwide.

Imagine a farmer who wants to transition to agroforestry. They might face upfront costs for planting trees, and a period of lower yields as the system establishes itself. Without [policy support Meaning → Policy support provides the necessary backing for developing and implementing policies that address sustainability challenges across environmental, social, and economic dimensions.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/policy-support/)  like financial incentives or technical assistance, this transition can be risky and difficult. Policies can **de-risk the transition** and make agroecology a viable and attractive option for farmers.

![A carefully arranged assembly of natural objects portrays the necessity of ecological harmony advocating for climate action. A stem acts as a foundation representing a robust environment supporting vulnerable elements like bone reflecting climate change impact. This arrangement underscores food security highlighting challenges exacerbated by biodiversity loss.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/ecological-balance-a-symbiotic-composition-illustrating-the-delicate-interplay.jpg "Ecological Balance a Symbiotic Composition Illustrating the Delicate Interplay in Our Ecosystem Promoting Environmental Awareness and Climate Action")

### Beyond Direct Support → Creating an Enabling Environment

But policy support for agroecology isn’t just about direct payments or programs. It’s also about creating a broader environment that is conducive to agroecological transitions. This means looking at policies across different sectors:

Think about school food programs. If policies mandated that schools source food from local agroecological farms, it would create a guaranteed market for these producers, educate children about sustainable food, and support local economies. This is an example of how policy can create a **virtuous cycle** that benefits farmers, consumers, and the environment.

In essence, [supporting agroecological transitions Supporting tradition ethically requires balancing cultural preservation with environmental sustainability and social justice.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/question/what-are-the-ethical-implications-of-supporting-tradition/)  globally requires a **holistic policy approach**. It’s not just about tweaking existing agricultural policies, but about rethinking our entire food system and aligning policies across sectors to create an environment where agroecology can flourish. It’s about building a future where food production is not just about quantity, but also about quality, equity, and sustainability.

![A still life of fruit and a budding branch in a ceramic cup symbolizes sustainability through minimalism. Natural biodegradable components champion resource conservation, addressing waste reduction efforts within personal aesthetics. The design choice promotes awareness of environmental impact and inspires low carbon lifestyle choices, reducing environmental degradation to encourage biodiversity and support sustainable agriculture.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-arrangements-with-natural-elements-showcasing-circular-economy.jpg "Sustainable Arrangements with Natural Elements Showcasing Circular Economy Ideals in Minimally Processed Materials and Biodegradable Art")

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the fundamentals, we begin to see the **complexity and multi-layered nature** of policy support for agroecological transitions. It’s not simply a matter of implementing a few straightforward policies. Effective support requires a deep understanding of the socio-economic, environmental, and political contexts in different regions, and a willingness to adapt policies to specific local needs and challenges.

The global landscape of agriculture is incredibly diverse, and a one-size-fits-all policy approach is unlikely to be successful. We need **context-specific solutions** that are tailored to the unique circumstances of different farming systems and communities.

Effective policy support for agroecology demands a nuanced understanding of diverse contexts and the development of tailored, localized solutions.

One key challenge is navigating the **political economy of food systems**. Industrial agriculture is deeply entrenched, with powerful vested interests that benefit from the status quo. [Agroecological transitions Meaning → Agroecological Transitions denote a shift in agricultural practices towards systems that mimic natural ecological processes.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/area/agroecological-transitions/)  often challenge these power structures, requiring policies that can overcome resistance from established actors and create space for alternative approaches.

This might involve addressing issues like corporate concentration in the food industry, lobbying power, and the influence of agribusiness on policy-making. **Political will and systemic change** are essential.

![A representation of a planet marred by environmental stressors. Encompassing this deteriorating sphere is a single ring. Two torus shaped items entwine around it symbolizing disruption.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-conceptualization-of-a-planets-fragility-ringed-by-environmental.jpg "Abstract Conceptualization of a Planet’s Fragility Ringed by Environmental Degradation Highlighting Climate Change Sustainability Concerns")

### What Are the Key Policy Instruments for Agroecological Transitions?

Policy instruments are the specific tools that governments and other actors can use to implement policies. In the context of agroecology, a range of instruments can be deployed:

Policy instruments, ranging from regulations to market mechanisms and participatory governance, must be strategically combined to drive agroecological transitions.

Consider the issue of pesticide use. Regulatory frameworks can restrict the use of highly toxic pesticides, while market-based mechanisms like eco-labels can reward farmers who use fewer pesticides or adopt integrated pest management strategies. [Participatory governance Meaning → Participatory Governance, within the context of sustainability, signifies a collaborative paradigm of decision-making.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/area/participatory-governance/)  can ensure that these policies are developed in consultation with farmers and other stakeholders, taking into account their knowledge and concerns. A **combination of instruments** is often more effective than relying on any single approach.

![A visual metaphor depicting the precarious equilibrium between nature and environmental damage. Smooth stones form a solid foundation being impacted by an ominous dark cloud emissions, representing unsustainable pollution impacting ecological integrity. The image prompts contemplation of environmental protection, biodiversity loss and the urgent need for circular economy practices and carbon footprint reductions and fostering carbon neutrality.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-balance-tensions-stones-pollution-metaphor-for-climate-change.jpg "Sustainable Balance Tensions Stones Pollution Metaphor for Climate Change Threat to Environmental Harmony")

### Addressing Trade-Offs and Synergies in Policy Design

Policy-making for agroecological transitions is not always straightforward. There can be trade-offs and potential conflicts between different policy objectives. For example, policies aimed at increasing food production in the short term might conflict with long-term [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth's biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  goals. It’s important to carefully consider these trade-offs and strive for policy synergies:

Think about the challenge of scaling up agroecology. Some argue that it is only suitable for small-scale farming and cannot be applied to larger, more intensive systems. However, agroecological principles can be adapted and applied across a range of farm sizes and production systems. Policies should support **innovative approaches to scaling up** agroecology, such as landscape-level approaches, farmer cooperatives, and value chain development.

Ultimately, supporting agroecological transitions globally requires a **systems thinking approach** to policy-making. It’s about recognizing the interconnectedness of food systems, ecosystems, and societies, and designing policies that address multiple challenges simultaneously. It’s about moving beyond narrow sectoral approaches and embracing a more holistic and integrated vision of sustainable agriculture and food systems.

![A splintered blue sphere impaled with metallic rods presents a strong visual statement on global warming and environmental destruction. The icy shards on its surface suggest the consequences of climate change like rising temperatures, ecosystem destruction and extreme weather events. The rods represent pollution, deforestation or extraction practices depleting resources impacting conservation efforts and sustainability transitions.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/frozen-globe-with-protruding-needles-a-metaphor-for-climate-change-impacts-and.jpg "Frozen Globe with Protruding Needles a Metaphor for Climate Change Impacts and Environmental Sustainability")

## Academic

From an academic lens, the question of how policy can support agroecological transitions globally moves into the realm of **complex systems theory**, **political ecology**, and **global governance**. Agroecology is not merely a set of farming techniques; it represents a fundamental shift in our understanding of agriculture and its relationship with society and the environment. Policy support, therefore, must go beyond technical fixes and address the deeper systemic issues that underpin unsustainable food systems.

This requires critical analysis of [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  dynamics, institutional structures, and the dominant paradigms that shape agricultural policy at local, national, and global levels. We need to move beyond incremental changes and consider **transformative pathways** towards agroecological futures.

Academic inquiry frames agroecological transitions as systemic transformations requiring policy to address power dynamics, institutional structures, and dominant paradigms.

A central theme in academic discourse is the concept of **food regime theory**, which highlights the historical and political forces that have shaped global food systems. Current food regimes are characterized by industrial agriculture, corporate control, and a focus on global commodity markets. Agroecological transitions represent a challenge to these dominant regimes, seeking to create more localized, equitable, and sustainable alternatives. Policy support must therefore be understood within this broader context of **regime change and power struggles**.

![A stack of stones conveys the fragility of climate equilibrium. Balanced against a muted landscape and sky, it speaks of sustainable balance as a method to deal with environmental impact from climate change. Its message promotes sustainability by urging viewers to be mindful of the need for renewable resources, promoting ecological balance, climate action, and conservation as global warming solutions in a time of increased climate resilience for our Earth system.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/balanced-stone-sculpture-symbolizing-environmental-harmony-and-sustainable.jpg "Balanced Stone Sculpture Symbolizing Environmental Harmony and Sustainable Development in a Changing Climate")

### What Are the Systemic Policy Shifts Needed for Global Agroecological Transitions?

To achieve widespread agroecological transitions, policy needs to address systemic barriers and create enabling conditions at multiple scales:

Systemic policy shifts for agroecology necessitate reforming global governance, decentralizing food systems, and challenging corporate power to foster food sovereignty.

Consider the role of intellectual property rights in agriculture. Current patent regimes often favor large seed companies and restrict farmers’ rights to save and exchange seeds, undermining agrobiodiversity and farmer autonomy. Policies that promote **farmers’ rights to seeds**, support open-source seed systems, and restrict the patenting of life forms are crucial for agroecological transitions.

![The composition captures a small ecosystem, emphasizing sustainable living. A fresh bloom arises beside dry plant matter, showing cycles in climate systems and nature's resilience. Complementing the composition, carefully placed rocks encourage a view on biodiversity, soil health and nature's sustainable resource management.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/ecosystem-balance-a-small-sustainable-world-with-natural-elements-and-climate.jpg "Ecosystem Balance a Small Sustainable World with Natural Elements and Climate Harmony Reflection")

### Analyzing the Socio-Ecological Outcomes of Policy Choices

Academic research emphasizes the importance of analyzing the broader socio-ecological outcomes of policy choices related to agroecological transitions. This goes beyond simply measuring agricultural yields or economic indicators, and considers a wider range of impacts:

Think about the long-term consequences of relying on synthetic fertilizers. While they may boost yields in the short term, they can also degrade soil health, pollute water sources, and contribute to greenhouse gas emissions in the long run. Policies should consider these **long-term socio-ecological costs** and prioritize agroecological alternatives that offer more sustainable and resilient pathways.

In conclusion, supporting agroecological transitions globally from an academic perspective demands a **critical and transformative policy agenda**. It requires challenging dominant paradigms, addressing power imbalances, and embracing a more holistic and systemic approach to [food systems Meaning → Food systems represent the complex network of activities involved in feeding a population: from production, processing, distribution, and retail, to consumption and waste disposal.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/area/food-systems/)  governance. It’s about creating policies that not only support agroecological practices, but also contribute to broader goals of social justice, environmental sustainability, and food sovereignty, ultimately fostering a more just and ecologically sound world.

#### Tags:

### Discover More

![Agroecology fosters resilient, equitable, and sustainable food systems for long-term environmental and societal well-being.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/what-are-the-long-term-impacts-of-agroecology-question-image.webp)

# [Department of Agroecology](/en)

![Aarhus University Seal](https://cdn.au.dk/2016/assets/img/au_segl-inv.svg)

### 

# Agroecology into policy - supporting the transition

# Tekstboks - Agroecology into policy – supporting the transition

### 

### **Agroecology can lead the way towards sustainable food and agriculture systems, according to many governments, international agencies and advocacy groups. However, supporting policies are needed to overcome context-specific barriers and for scaling up agroecological practices.**

### 

*By Chiara de Notaris, *Saghar Motarjemi*, Johannes (Jeroen) Pullens and Claudia Windeck*

Agroecology is based on the integration between scientific knowledge, local traditions and social principles. This holistic approach is central to achieve sustainable food and agriculture systems, and enables producers and communities to develop context-specific solutions to local challenges.

Several institutions at national and international level have recognized the value of agroecology and FAO has identified 10 elements to guide “policymakers, practitioners and stakeholders in planning, managing and evaluating agroecological transitions”.   
  
FAO´s 10 Elements of Agroecology are:

Overall, agroecology is seen as a key to meeting many of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the United Nations, such as zero hunger, no poverty and responsible consumption and production. In order to achieve its potential, agroecological practices need to become mainstream on a broad scale. The implementation of supportive policies can help to overcome context-specific barriers, facilitating the scaling-up of agroecology. France and Brazil provide examples of such policies.

### **Context-specific priorities**

In the French national policy, agroecology is identified as a tool for sustainable intensification, which is a related approach aiming at increasing agricultural production while minimizing environmental impacts, “based on increasing farmers’ skills in managing the ecosystems their farm represents”. Agroecological practices are seen as a fusion of scientific and traditional knowledge, with a clear emphasis on the economic dimensions and less on the social. Innovation is a focus point of the French Agroecology Project, which is based on incentives to encourage farmers to embrace agroecology.

A different approach is used in the Brazilian National Policy for Agroecology and Organic Production (PNAPO). Here the social dimension is core, and it emphasizes rural development and family farming. The policy is the product of a process started in the ’70s, when peasants’ social movements adopted agroecology as a form of resistance to the Green Revolution model. The long history of agroecology in Brazil led to the creation of many advocacy groups, including SOCLA (Latin American Scientific Society of Agroecology). It aims at promoting the “science of agroecology as the scientific basis of a sustainable rural development strategy in Latin America” and keeps a strong focus on food sovereignty and conservation of natural resources.

These were just two examples of how agroecology can be integrated into policy, in coherence with local needs. The recognition of national and international institutions will push the movement of transition towards sustainable food and agricultural systems further.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Supportive policies are crucial to redirect resources toward agroecological practices, ensuring financial incentives and technical assistance for farmers transitioning from industrial to sustainable farming methods.

Collaboration among farmers, governments, NGOs, and local communities fosters knowledge exchange and shared resources, enhancing the implementation of agroecological practices and strengthening local food systems.
